summary: Controls EnablePlainTextPassword under LanmanWorkstation. Disabling plaintext SMB passwords is a normal hardening step on modern systems, but very old third-party SMB servers can still depend on it.

setting.name: Plaintext SMB Passwords
setting.casual_explanation: This setting decides whether Windows is willing to send an unencrypted password to certain old SMB servers. Modern systems should almost never need that behavior.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable plaintext SMB passwords for security. They only enable it when they must talk to very old third-party SMB servers that cannot handle safer authentication.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning plaintext SMB passwords off is safer and usually the right choice. Turning it on can help with very old servers, but it weakens password protection on the network.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents EnablePlainTextPassword under HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanWorkstation\Parameters. A value of 0 disables sending unencrypted passwords, while 1 allows it.

allowed_values.label: Windows managed baseline
allowed_values.meaning: Windows uses its built-in baseline for plaintext SMB password behavior.

allowed_values.label: Do not send plaintext passwords
allowed_values.meaning: Windows does not send unencrypted passwords to third-party SMB servers.

allowed_values.label: Allow plaintext passwords
allowed_values.meaning: Windows can send unencrypted passwords to third-party SMB servers.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes EnablePlainTextPassword = 0, which matches the documented safer baseline.

windows_defaults.label: Windows managed SMB client baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows SMB client releases with LanmanWorkstation plaintext password support

states.rationale: Leave the explicit override absent and let Windows keep its own baseline.

windows_defaults.notes: This record documents the official behavior of the value but does not force a single historical Windows default across all releases.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows managed default
intended_for: Users who want Windows to keep its normal SMB client baseline
People auditing an environment before forcing a hardening override
avoid_for: Security baselines that explicitly forbid plaintext SMB passwords
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Windows to keep its own baseline until you decide whether a hardening override is necessary.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a clear local rule that forbids plaintext SMB passwords.
tradeoffs: You are not explicitly hardening this specific SMB behavior.

recommended_profiles.label: Modern secure baseline
avoid_for: Very old third-party SMB servers that still need plaintext passwords

states.rationale: Do not allow Windows to send unencrypted passwords to SMB servers.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the app to force the safer SMB client behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You already know a legacy SMB server still depends on plaintext passwords.
tradeoffs: Some old SMB servers may stop working.

recommended_profiles.label: Legacy third-party server
intended_for: Old SMB servers that truly need plaintext passwords

states.rationale: Allow plaintext SMB passwords only for a confirmed legacy requirement.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You have confirmed that an old third-party SMB server cannot work without plaintext password support.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You are enabling it just in case without a real compatibility need.
tradeoffs: Password protection on the network becomes weaker.

evidence.title: Microsoft Policy CSP: LocalPoliciesSecurityOptions / plaintext SMB passwords
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents EnablePlainTextPassword for the Windows network client security option and defines 0 as disabled and 1 as enabled.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft security metadata: sceregvl.inf
evidence.summary: Microsoft security metadata maps EnablePlainTextPassword to HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanWorkstation\Parameters.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes EnablePlainTextPassword = 0 for the current tweak.

decision.why: Microsoft security metadata maps EnablePlainTextPassword to the exact LanmanWorkstation registry path, and Microsoft Policy CSP documentation provides the 0/1 meaning used by the app. The current implementation writes the documented safer value 0.

validation_proof.notes: Local official Microsoft security metadata confirms the exact registry path and value name. The Microsoft Policy CSP page for 'Microsoft network client: Send unencrypted password to connect to third-party SMB servers' provides the numeric semantics: Default Value 0, Allowed values 1 Enable and 0 (Default) Disable.
